data.title: Rates Spark: ECB Unlikely To Trigger Any Directionality In Rates
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Benjamin Schroeder

Both markets and the ECB need more data to decide on direction

Heading into Thursday’s European Central Bank meeting, the market is almost fully priced for a 25bp cut. This means the market will be mainly looking for hints about future rate trajectories during the press conference. A downwardly revised inflation projection and a balance of risks around the growth outlook, perhaps more clearly pointing to the downside, would generally paint a dovish backdrop, but given the uncertain environment around trade specifically, one should not expect any concrete hints from President Lagarde.

The market still anticipates at least one more cut by the end of the year, bringing the deposit facility rate to 1.75%. There is a moderate chance of further cuts, but pricing is largely influenced by sentiment around US-EU trade relations these days and its feed through to activity this side of the Atlantic - the US data on Wednesday did not move the needle here at all.

Between the tariff news and Germany’s fiscal U-turn in March, markets have established a range for the terminal deposit facility rate of 1.5-2%. Currently, at the lower end of this range, a slightly dovish tone from the ECB now is unlikely to change the overall picture, keeping pricing in the lower half of the range. A significant drop in rates would likely require a series of weaker data points. However, the downside in rates is limited by the prospect of increased EU defence spending and fiscal stimulus from Germany.

US rates wary of worsening data but euro rates relatively unfazed

A day like Wednesday highlights the disentanglement between US and euro rates. Where the 10Y UST dove some 7bp lower on poor jobs numbers and ISM data, the 10Y euro swap rate remained unfazed close to 2.5%. The short end of the US curve is in a difficult position because the prices paid component of the ISM survey rose significantly in May, potentially already reflecting the impact of tariffs. Risk sentiment seemed to keep up, perhaps because the Friday US payroll numbers are more the focus.

In an environment where euro rates seem detached from US Treasury dynamics, the landing zone of the ECB plays an even more important role in setting the 10Y EUR swap rate. The front end of the swap curve also, however, shows little movement, simply because the uncertain environment doesn’t really dictate whether the ECB will have to cut below 1.75%. A faltering US economy could, in our view, weigh quite heavily on the global economy and risk sentiment. If we therefore see more bad US numbers, from e.g. Friday’s payrolls, then we would expect a more material move lower in euro rates than witnessed on Wednesday.

Thursday's events and market views

The ECB meeting is the main event for EUR rates, but with markets fully priced for a cut, the reaction to such an outcome should be muted, in particular if the ECB offers little further guidance.

US data in the run-up to Friday’s payrolls report could prove more market-moving, at least for US rates. The weekly jobless claims had risen somewhat last week. If the anticipated bounce back does not materialise, it could add to the gloom instilled by the ISM on Wednesday. From the Fed, we will hear from speakers Kugler, Harker and Schmid. In politics, we will be watching the White House visit of German Chancellor Merz.

France and Spain will be active with bond auctions. Spain sells 3y to 7y bonds and 15y linkers. France sells long-end bonds from 10y to 30y maturities.

data.title: The AES Corporation Remains Unscathed By Tariffs And Economic Downturns
data.description:
I have covered the AES Corporation before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it a strong buy. Since then, the stock has returned almost -20% and has reported a disappointing Q1 result. However, it is still signing new projects which indicate that companies are still interested in its vision.

Background

The AES Corporation (NYSE:AES) is a global energy company focused on green energy, and with its main operations in the United States. The company also has a utility segment with operations in several US states.

AES has a strong pipeline of new projects of 11.7 GW, which ensures a strong growth at least until 202. This growth is expected to propel earnings higher and the company is delivering the projects on time, which means that most growth will already be online by 2027. Moreover, most of this growth comes from big tech clients with attractive PPAs.

But even more interesting is that two thirds of Adjusted EBITDA is from long term contracted generation that is take or pay and not tied to demand. Moreover, the company has just a small exposure to tariffs, with most of the equipment already in the US or contracted for domestic supply.

There is a small exposure on energy storage; however, the batteries come from outside China, which could limit the impact if there are trade deals.

Management also reiterated its 2025 guidance and the capital allocation plan, which expects to distribute $1,640 million in dividends until 2027. Right now, the company market cap is around $7.3 billion, which represents that the company will pay more than a 22% of its current market cap.

Finally, as mentioned in my previous article, it is important to remember that the company takes a lot of debt while constructing a new project. When the project is completed, tax attributes are compensated, and debt is reduced by more than half. Due to the elevated growth, this has an important impact and around 20% of the current debt is from projects under construction that doesn’t provide any cashflow.

Financial position and Valuation

At first glance, it seems that AES has an enormous amount of debt, with more than $29 billion and below $3 billion expected EBITDA for 2025. However, as mentioned before, around 20% of its debt is from projects under construction that will be reduced in half when the assets start operations and at the same time will increase EBITDA and cash flow. Moreover, most of the debt is non-recourse, which also limits the risks. Management also commented that they have already refinanced all of the parent 2025 debt maturities.

Q1 results were below consensus; however, management reiterated its 2025 and long-term guidance. 2025 guidance, which expects between $2.1 and $2.25 EPS, suggest the stock trades below a 5 P/E ratio before all the expected growth, considerably below its historical valuation.

The reaffirmed long-term guidance suggests that 2027 EBITDA will be around $3,400 million and FCF around $1,200 million. To be conservative, I have assumed 2027 EBITDA to be $3,143 million, which is 2024 EBITDA with a 6% annual increase. Maintaining the current EV/EBITDA valuation, 2027 share price should more than double to $24 per share.

This is due to two effects, the increase in EBITDA and the reduction in debt resulting from the commencement of the projects currently under construction. This is why I believe the stock is very attractive at around $10 per share.

Risks

The main risk could be changes in the US legislation. However, as stated in the conference call, US demand has remained strong despite uncertainty. Moreover, there are Safe Harbor protections which enable continued growth for now. Finally, management commented that their international renewable projects which do not rely on tax credits have higher returns than in the US projects.

Another risk is the elevated debt and rates. While most of the debt is non-recourse, the company needs to issue new debt regularly, and with current interest rates this is a burden.

For example, the company issued a new 5.8% 2032 note to repay the 3.3% 2025 notes. This represents a significant increase in cost that will slightly reduce profitability.

Conclusion

The AES Corporation remains an attractive investment with the potential to double in value over the next few years. The significant growth expected in the coming years, along with some debt reduction, should allow the stock to trade higher. I believe the fair value is around $24 per share, which represents an upside potential of more than 100%.

Moreover, the company is expected to pay out more than 22% of its current market cap as dividends by 2027.

There is some uncertainty regarding legislation; however, for now, the impact seems minimal and should not affect the long-term demand for green energy.

data.title: Diamond Hill Mid Cap Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Markets were mixed in Q1 as the new presidential administration made repeated waves with an avalanche of activity, which left markets uncertain of the likely path forward. For the quarter, US stocks fell -4.7% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index), notching markets’ first quarterly decline since Q3 2023. Mid-cap stocks held up best, falling a more modest -3.4%, while large caps were down -4.5%. Smaller stocks were hit harder, falling -9.5% — a relatively unsurprising development given smaller companies’ shares are generally more volatile as macroeconomic uncertainty increases. From a style perspective, value fared better up and down the cap spectrum, with large-cap value managing a positive gain of +2.1%. Mid-cap value stocks fell -2.1%, compared to -7.1% for their growth counterparts, while small-cap value stocks declined -7.7% versus -11.1% for small growth (all returns as measured by their respective Russell indices). The shift from growth to value was also relatively unsurprising given the flight to safety prompted by tremendous uncertainty around trade and other fiscal policy shifts in the US.

From a sector perspective, likewise, more defensive sectors in the Russell Midcap Index led in the quarter, while cyclical stocks fell out of favor. Energy stocks were the best performers, up +7.2% amid supportive language from President Trump’s administration about its willingness to increase US oil production. Utilities also rose nicely, up +6.8%. Conversely, technology stocks were hit hardest, falling -9.9% — which, given the sector’s weight in the index, was a major contributor to the index’s negative Q1 return. Consumer discretionary stocks also fell -8.2%.

What began in Q4 — market leadership transitions in anticipation of potential winners and losers from a major shift in executive-level policy direction — vigorously continued in Q1. To some extent, economic data took a backseat in the quarter as investors seemed to focus narrowly on the likeliest implications of major trade policy shifts announced during the quarter by President Trump. Shortly after the quarter concluded, Trump announced a raft of tariffs to go into effect immediately on what he dubbed “Liberation Day.” As has been the case since Trump began increasing tariffs, countries are responding in various ways, with some reaching out to the US to negotiate better rates and others — notably, China — choosing to retaliate with their own high tariff rates. Many companies have preemptively announced significant investments in their US operations — ostensibly moves aimed at avoiding tariffs themselves. Ultimately, whether the “Liberation Day” moniker goes down in history or infamy remains to be seen. What is clear is markets are very nervous about the president’s apparent determination to wholesale (and possibly singlehandedly) rework the global trade landscape, even with countries that have historically been significant trade allies, like Taiwan. We’ll have more thoughts on potential direction from here later in this communication.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve held rates in March, citing the likelihood tariffs increase inflationary pressures, while cutting its growth expectations for US GDP in 2025. And the US dollar — which one would generally expect to be bolstered relative to other global currencies as tariffs go up (higher prices for US goods increase demand for US dollars with which to pay for them) — counterintuitively fell as tariff language toughened, perhaps signaling investors’ growing concerns tariffs will contribute to a stagflationary US economic environment with high inflation and low or sluggish growth.

Globally, geopolitical uncertainty remains high, though there have been some signs of modest progress toward ending the war between Russia and Ukraine. Following a dramatic fall-out between President Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the US and Ukraine seemed to slowly make their way back toward a minerals and mining deal which would meaningfully increase the US’s economic involvement in Ukraine and potentially pave the way for a broader peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. However, to date, Russian President Vladimir Putin has stymied the US’s attempts to broker a ceasefire between the two countries. War in the Middle East continues, too, as ceasefires between Israel and Hamas and Israel and Lebanon ended and violence across the region re- escalated. The US, too, struck Houthi rebels in Yemen toward the end of the quarter in an attempt to quell attacks on Red Sea shipping lanes.

While volatility is unsettling, it also introduces interesting investing opportunities for those who have been diligent in their research all along. We have no unique insight into how deep this correction will become, whether it will enter bear market territory or how long it will last. However, we know it has helped reset valuations and offered some compelling opportunities to purchase high-quality names at more attractive prices than we’ve seen in some time. Once the trade-related dust settles, time will tell which sectors and individual companies are winners and losers. In the meantime, we will continue deploying our time-tested approach, which is well-built for uncertain market environments like we’re currently seeing — and which we wouldn’t be surprised to see persist for the foreseeable future.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio outperformed the Russell Midcap Index in Q1. Relative strength was heavily concentrated among our financials holdings, which delivered positive returns in a quarter in which benchmark peers were in the red. Our technology holdings — as well as our below-benchmark exposure to the underperforming sector — were also a tailwind. Conversely, our energy holdings, while positive on an absolute basis, trailed benchmark names, weighing on relative performance in Q1. Our below-benchmark exposure to utilities also posed a modest headwind — though our holdings were nicely positive.

On an individual holdings basis, top contributors to return in Q1 included Verisign (VRSN) and Huntington Ingalls (HII). VeriSign, which provides internet infrastructure and domain name registry services, successfully renewed its .com agreement with the US Department of Commerce in late 2024. It also demonstrated stability in both its .com and .net domain bases. We believe the company is favorably positioned with an attractive business model and management team, which has effectively opportunistically allocated capital.

Shares of US Department of Defense and Navy supplier Huntington Ingalls rose in Q1 following President Trump’s announced formation of a new office of shipbuilding. The president further made comments suggesting the government is working to help Huntington Ingalls address recent supply chain and labor issues, which we anticipate will be resolved over time. We maintain our conviction in Huntington Ingalls’ positioning relative to the Department of Defense’s need to prepare for a potential conflict in the Pacific — which would in turn ensure strong demand for the next 5 to 10 years. Further, we believe resolving the aforementioned supply chain and labor issues will allow Huntington Ingalls to improve its margins — a likelihood which we believe is not yet reflected in the share price.

Other top contributors in Q1 included Mr. Cooper Group (COOP), Boston Scientific (BSX) and American International Group (AIG). Mortgage servicing company Mr. Cooper Group executed well during a particularly challenging mortgage environment as interest rates have remained high relative to historical levels. Importantly, Mr. Cooper’s business model is specifically designed to help protect it from just such periods: As rates go up, the value of its mortgage-servicing rights business goes up, giving it the ability to continue generating healthy cash flows even as the mortgage industry overall may slow. Shares of medical device manager Boston Scientific rose in the quarter as it continues capitalizing on demographic tailwinds to drive growth across its businesses.

Property and casualty insurance company AIG is delivering high-quality results: Its most recently reported underlying loss ratio was better than expected, as were catastrophe losses and prior-year development. Further, the company continues generating revenue growth from both its commercial and consumer segments. We are pleased with the progress AIG is making to streamline its business and capitalize on opportunities for earnings growth better than its competitors. We believe it remains well-positioned to scale revenues efficiently and deliver sustainable value as it continues reducing expenses and returning excess capital via share repurchases.

Among our bottom Q1 individual contributors were Regal Rexnord (RRX) and WESCO (WCC). Power transmission components manufacturer Regal Rexnord faced pressure amid growing tariff-related concerns. Generally, its organic growth has held up better than peers’, and though its leverage is somewhat elevated, merger synergies and debt paydown rates are beating expectations — factors which should position it well relative to competitors. We believe the recent weakness has been an overreaction and maintain our conviction in our thesis.

Shares of industrial distributor WESCO were pressured amid the company’s cyclical exposure and leveraged balance sheet. However, despite recent mixed fundamentals, we believe the company can still leverage its significant scale advantage to take share and improve margins over time. Further, we expect it to benefit from several secular tailwinds, which should contribute to strong free cash-flow generation, allowing it to deleverage over time.

Other bottom individual contributors in Q1 included Ciena (CIEN), Teleflex (TFX) and Ashland (ASH). Shares of networking systems company Ciena were pressured amid concerns about the durability and timing of cloud customers’ orders. Teleflex, which designs, develops, manufactures and supplies single- use medical devices for common critical care and surgical diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, declined following its announcement that it would spin off some of its underperforming businesses.

Shares of specialty chemical manufacturer Ashland have been pressured by dour sentiment, even as earnings have generally met expectations. The company has indicated that potential tariffs should not particularly affect it, given that most of its production locations and end markets are well-matched within countries. Regardless, investors seem relatively unconvinced and are possibly also factoring in broader economic growth concerns.

Portfolio Activity

As volatility picked up sharply in the quarter, we were active in the portfolio — and we anticipate that as volatility continues into Q2, we will likewise attempt to capitalize on compelling opportunities to reposition the portfolio for the period ahead. We initiated four new positions in Q1: Martin Marietta Materials (MLM), Ventas (VTR), Illumina (ILMN) and TransUnion (TRU).

Martin Marietta Materials is a heavy building materials supplier and the second-largest aggregates producer and distributor in the US with competitively positioned cement, ready-mix, asphalt and magnesia specialty businesses. The company has a long-term mindset, which proved effective during the last recession. Generally, the company tries to be the price leader in its markets and has historically successfully passed inflationary prices along to its clients.

Given its strategic positioning in markets with strong growth potential and in states with approved multi-year Department of Transportation plans, we believe it is well-positioned for the period ahead. Recent macroeconomic uncertainty has pressured shares, introducing a compelling opportunity for us to invest in a quality aggregates company at a good entry point.

Ventas is a diversified health care real estate investment trust (REIT) focused on private-pay senior housing — primarily independent and assisted living — as well as outpatient medical offices and research/life sciences. The demographics around senior housing are compelling over the medium term: The 80+ age cohort in the US is rapidly growing, while senior housing is limited, which should drive years of strong growth as the industry recovers from oversupply and post-COVID weakness. We anticipate occupancy should improve meaningfully, while pricing is likely to remain solid as senior care is a needs-based business. At approximately 85% occupancy, a facility generally requires full staffing, making additional tenants beyond that occupancy level significantly higher margin. We also don’t anticipate significant new facility development — and even when it starts, it could take some time before annual new construction catches up with demand. Further, recently weaker fundamentals among competitors could create attractive acquisition opportunities for Ventas. Finally, the company’s operating platform and pricing software should bring institutional sophistication to a business that has long been tech-averse, giving it a significant and growing competitive advantage. Given we don’t believe the valuation adequately reflects these advantages, we initiated a position in Q1.

Illumina provides sequencing- and array-based solutions for genetic and genomic analysis. It has roughly 80% share in its markets, strong fundamentals and a healthy balance sheet. Looking forward, we believe optionality from new tests other companies run using Illumina products, particularly for cancer screening and detection, should create a long-term growth tailwind. Shares have been pressured recently amid weaker end-market demand, combined with concerns about China, new competition from competitor Roche and the potential for National Institutes of Health funding cuts — giving us an opportunity to initiate an investment in a quality business with good long-term growth prospects at the forefront of genetic sequencing.

TransUnion is a global consumer credit reporting agency providing risk and information solutions. Recent concerns about higher interest rates and the risk of recession have weighed on shares, giving us an opportunity to start a position. We believe the market is overly focused on the near term and believe the long-term outlook for this company is solid.

We funded these purchases in part with the proceeds from our sales of regional airline Alaska Air Group (ALK), property and casualty insurance provider Loews (L), welding and cutting equipment and consumables provider ESAB, timberland REIT Rayonier (RYN), apartment REIT UDR and electronics component manufacturer Sensata Technologies (ST) — all of which we sold in favor of more compelling investment opportunities elsewhere.

We also exited our position in Informatica, a comprehensive data management software company providing an array of solutions that facilitate monitoring, manipulating and moving data assets at large enterprises. Following Q4, we had serious questions about management’s ability to execute Informatica’s cloud model transition, as well as about the underlying strength of the business’s installed base. We consequently sold our position to reallocate our capital to names in which we have greater fundamental conviction.

Market Outlook

As we start Q2, uncertainty is as high as it has been, arguably since the pandemic’s earliest days. While markets struggle to digest the likeliest ramifications of the US’s newly announced tariffs — whether they are largely a negotiation tactic whose economic impact will moderate over time as countries and companies reach better terms with the US, or whether they are here to stay for the long term and we will indeed see a significant reshaping of the world trade order — we are watching several factors particularly closely. Foremost, we are monitoring the relationship between the US and China, which is front and center in this trade showdown. As we write, President Trump has threatened a 104% tariff on China in response to its retaliatory increase to 84%. Given the magnitude of the US and Chinese economies, seeing the two countries enter a full-blown trade war would have significant, widespread implications for many US companies, up and down the capitalization spectrum.

Further, the increased uncertainty across a range of macroeconomic policies — trade as well as domestic spending and the administration’s ongoing efforts to meaningfully shrink the size of the federal government — is prompting concerns about the consumer. Should tariffs prompt a significant spike in inflation and/or fiscal spending cuts go too far, consumers could rein in their spending.

Indeed, companies across various sectors have noted a weakening consumer, suggesting the economy is slowing. With the consumer powering a significant majority of the economy, further weakening may present a risk to the lofty double-digit corporate earnings growth the market had been expecting for 2025.

However, regardless of how uncomfortable such volatility can be, we are simultaneously encouraged by its impact on the valuations of some companies that we believe are particularly strong and well-positioned to weather the environment ahead — even if it requires further suffering before economic and market growth resume. Accordingly, we have sought to be active in this period, identifying high- quality companies with particularly solid balance sheets whose outlook over the next three to five years is positive in our view. As downward volatility has continued, we have further acted on our research efforts to introduce these names to the portfolio, with the aim of positioning the portfolio well for whatever the environment gives us in the period ahead — whether there is further downside to endure, or whether markets turn around precipitously. We believe, with hindsight, that this period may prove to be one that separates those investors with a rigorous, bottom-up process from those unwilling or unable to see through the near-term volatility to the possibilities beyond.

Our primary focus is always on achieving value-added results for our existing clients, and we believe we can achieve better-than-market returns over the next five years through active portfolio management.

data.title: Ternium: Latin Steelmaker Betting Big On Nearshoring - And It's Dirt Cheap
data.description:
Summary

Ternium S.A. (NYSE:TX) is a vertically integrated steel producer with a strong presence in Central America. Backed by Techint Group, one of the region's largest industrial conglomerates, TX stands out from its operational expertise and disciplined capital allocation. I believe TX can capture share from the structural short flat steel market due to the opportunity to reduce its steel import dependence and accelerating industrial nearshoring. With TX trading at just 7.1x forward P/E with a 9% dividend yield, TX can gain when the capital and steel cycles turn. It is a Buy for me at these levels.

Business Model

TX has an integrated business model that covers from raw material sourcing, steel production, downstream processing and distribution. Its operations are centered around Central and South America countries such as Brazil and Colombia. TX mainly produces flat and long steel products, serving a diversified end-markets such as to automotive manufacturers and appliance makers.

TX sources iron ore in Mexico and Brazil and turns them into steel. It makes steel through Basic Oxygen Furnaces (BOF) and Electric Arc Furnaces (EAF) as it is not reliant on the traditional steel making procedure. The BOF is the traditional, coke-based way of steelmaking. Its key advantage is its lower cost per ton, particularly when iron ore is cheap. TX source its ore from the Las Encinas mine in Mexico, making its BOF operations more cost competitive. The downside is that it has high fixed cost with high breakeven utilization of >80%, and carbon intensive.

Next, EAF is another steelmaking method that melts scrap steel and impurities are removed. The pros of EAF are that it is cheaper and faster to build than BOF factories and is significantly less carbon intensive. However, it relies on scrap supply which may be difficult to obtain and is highly sensitive to power prices.

TX's facilities are positioned near high-growth markets in Latin America and the US. This is beneficial especially with the nearshoring trends where companies are relocating their supply chain closer to North America due to US-China trade tensions and USMCA incentives. Moreover, Mexico imports 48% of its steel from overseas. This means there is an opportunity to capture from import substitution.

Therefore, TX's business model is well-integrated from its access to cheap raw materials, value-added product focus and operational flexibility, allowing it to maintain cost leadership amongst its peers.

Investment Thesis

Currently, TX presents a mid-cycle investment opportunity, supported by structural supply-demand tailwinds in the Latin America steel market. TX is one of the few producers that deploy strategic growth capital into high-return, ESG aligned projects. These efforts are aimed to capturing the import substitution opportunities. This is important because Latin America's steel sector had a decade of underinvestment from global overcapacity in Asia even when steel prices spiked. But TX is only one of the few that makes strategic bets on cleaner steel production while the Mexico and Brazil governments would safeguard local manufacturing.

For instance, the firm deployed ~$4B capex program at Mexico on EAF. This means that TX would be ahead of the curve when other producers are cautious in building new capacity due to their fear of subsidized steel from Asia.

As per the capital cycle framework, the steel industry goes through phases of boom, bust, underinvestment and reinvestment. During boom periods, high prices attract capital, which leads to overcapacity. But this is followed by a bust where profits collapse and capital becomes scarce again. Currently, Latin America is now in the stage of underinvestment as the demand is met by low-cost imports from Asia, especially China. But TX is at the final stage of reinvestment ahead of its peers. With nearshoring and trade protections against Asia grow, local customers would prefer local steel. Thus, this creates a timing advantage where TX could reap the benefits quicker when demand returns while its peers are still investing to build new capacity.

Moreover, nearshoring is gaining momentum. For example, foreign direct investments (FDI) to Mexico had reached a record of $21.4bn in 1Q 2025. With nearing shoring waves would support long-term demand for steel. TX would be well-positioned to capture a disproportionate share from this investment influx.

Valuation

TX appears to be significantly undervalued across almost all metrics. For example, its forward P/E is trading at 7.1x and that is 56% cheaper than the sector median. Its forward P/B is also trading at 0.45x which is a big discount of ~70% compared to the sector median. Moreover, the company has a net cash balance sheet and is also offering a significantly high dividend yield which is 3x higher than its peers.

Thus, these depressed multiples reflect a very big mispricing, and I believe this is due to the market's belief that the steel industry is past peak earnings, especially after the 2021-2022 price surge driven by spike in energy cost and Russia-Ukraine conflict. Moreover, investors are also putting a political risk premium, FX volatility on TX's core operations in the Latin America and lastly worrying that the huge capex would be dilutive to free cash flow and dividends. Whilst all these worries are not misplaced, the market is forgetting that the company has been paying dividends consistently since 2007 and has only grown its dividends per share since then. Moreover, the company has been net cash since 2021. Also, the market is not pricing in the amount of demand that can be absorbed by Ternium's new steel capacity in 2026 from imports. The volume growth would directly drive to TX's earnings.

Dividend Analysis

As the company is paying an attractive 9.4% dividend yield, it is important to check if they have the necessary cash to pay out their dividends overtime. Just by look at Seeking Alpha's latest payout ratio, it is "NM". This is because the EPS is negative for the year, but if we look across time, they usually range between 40-80% even for a cyclical business. The confidence in sustaining dividends is also backed by CFO Pablo Brizzio in the Q1 2025 earnings where they aim to sustain dividend payments, despite these difficult times.

Therefore, even though the payout levels are not fully covered by earnings but if after digging into the financials, the loss was attributed due to change in fair value of financial assets for FY24. This means the loss is not from operating reasons. The company is also net cash position and management's confidence suggests payout ratio would be secured in the middle term.

Risk

TX faces key risks such as steel price volatility as lower flat steel prices would compress margins. Chinese import competition still poses risk if anti-dumping measures are ineffective. Ternium has actually initiated anti-dumping investigations and lobbied for protective tariff.

Conclusion

TX offers a combination of undervaluation, strategic reinvestment and favourable trends for the company to turnaround. I think TX is quietly positioning itself for long-term margin expansion and ROIC improvement through its capex project. I believe this offers a compelling opportunity and I rate this a Buy.

data.title: If The Goal Isn't Price Stability, Then What Are These New 50% Tariffs Really About?
data.description:
Original Post

By Stuart Burns

Originally published on June 4, 2025

During a rally at U.S. Steel's (X) Mon Valley Works-Irvin Plant near Pittsburgh last Friday, U.S. President Trump announced plans to raise tariffs on steel and aluminum imports from 25% to 50%. These new "Trump Tariffs" are set to take effect on June 4, 2025, and will not only apply to primary and semi-finished steel and aluminum but also to derivative products - items that contain these metals, such as automotive parts, machinery components, and other manufactured goods.

Trump claimed the tariff hike would "further secure the steel industry in the United States." Many domestic steel and aluminum producers immediately welcomed the move, including Jesse Gary, President and CEO of Century Aluminum (CENX), the nation's largest primary aluminum producer. Gary responded by announcing plans to build "the first new aluminum smelter in the U.S. in 50 years" and to double domestic production.

"Trump Tariffs" Are Nothing New

Whether any of these plans come to fruition remains uncertain. During Trump's first term, he introduced similar tariffs with the stated goal of supporting domestic investment, but actual investment remained modest. While short-term fluctuations certainly occurred, production largely followed broader economic cycles and global steel market trends.

It is likewise important to note that building a steel or aluminum mill typically takes nearly a decade, from planning to rolling, and these facilities require a life expectancy of 30-40 years. Meanwhile, Trump - and potentially his current trade policies - could be gone in four years. This could happen even sooner if he succeeds in achieving what many believe to be his true goal: improving terms with America's trading partners.

Tariffs as Trade Leverage

There is an alternative argument circulating about the broader strategy behind the use of Section 232 tariffs on steel and aluminum. European leaders have referred to Trump's strategy as "bazooka diplomacy." The strategy largely uses tariffs as blunt-force leverage to extract concessions or policy changes from trading partners. His aim appears to be less about long-term protection of domestic industries and more about forcing countries to change their trade practices, or, in the case of America's closer neighbors, to address broader issues like migration or drug trafficking.

As a negotiating tactic, this strategy will likely prove effective with partners such as the EU, UK, Japan, and South Korea, all of whom are already in talks with U.S. trade officials to mitigate or avoid these sweeping tariffs. It's likely any resolution will require some mutual adjustment of tariff levels.

For years, the U.S. has faced a fundamental imbalance in tariff structures with major trade partners, so some degree of rebalancing may be long overdue. However, if rebalancing is the true intent behind Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs, then domestic producers could be making a mistake by investing under the assumption that the new 50% Trump tariffs, or even the previous 25%, will persist long term.

No major U.S. trading partner currently imposes such steep duties on U.S. steel and aluminum. So, if tariffs ultimately align with international norms, rates could fall back to the low single digits. If this happens, domestic producers banking on long-term protection may find themselves overextended.

Could Tariffs Represent a Long-Term Solution?

A good friend of mine often says, "Don't take Trump literally, take him seriously." By this, they mean, don't fixate on his exact words, but recognize that he's serious about pursuing strategic objectives. That's proven to be wise advice. But in this case, it's difficult to determine what Trump's core objective truly is.

Is he trying to shore up the domestic steel and aluminum industry for the long haul? If so, high-double digit tariffs may stick around. Is he using these tariffs as leverage for broader trade negotiations? In that case, they are likely to be dramatically scaled back if new deals are struck.

Despite industry promises of large-scale investment, few of those plans will likely materialize if tariffs drop back to single digits. Still, boosting domestic production has remained a central goal for the Trump team since day one, so Section 232 tariffs may well stay in place. Even if they don't remain at 50%, a moderately elevated rate may become the new normal. Meanwhile, broader tariffs on other goods likely fall under the "trade negotiation" category and could be waived or sharply reduced as trade agreements are finalized.

The UK Planning Countermeasures

But a development this week suggests that even Section 232 tariffs could be more about negotiation than permanence. The UK is currently negotiating the U.S.-UK Economic Prosperity Deal (EPD), which holds the potential for minimal or zero tariffs between the two countries. In reality, there's little trade imbalance - if anything, the UK runs a trade deficit with the U.S. Yet, this week, the Trump administration announced the UK would not face the additional 25% increase to 50% on steel and aluminum while negotiations continue. That move strongly suggests that favorable trade agreements could limit the lifespan of the higher tariffs.

One thing that is certain is that Trump has consistently prioritized his domestic audience. While it seems almost inconceivable that he would saddle American consumers with 50% Section 232 tariffs indefinitely, we may have to accept that high-double digit tariffs on steel and aluminum could be part of the long-term landscape, even as tariffs on other goods gradually decline. If I were a betting man, I wouldn't place high odds on Century Aluminum's promise to double domestic primary aluminum production

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ULTY: This Weekly Paying Option ETF Is For Risk Takers
data.description:
Overview

I've become quite fond of a few weekly paying option ETFs offered by YieldMax. However, I've always had mixed feelings about YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY) due to its continuous price declines in the past. Nearly a year ago, I initially covered ULTY and issued a hold rating due to uncertainty around its performance and the efficiency of its strategy. Now that a year has passed, and I've become more familiar with these sorts of high-yield funds, I wanted to revisit ULTY to provide an updated look at the value proposition and measure the performance of the fund.

ULTY deploys option strategies against an always-changing list of securities that typically have higher volatility. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that ULTY's price has rapidly deteriorated by nearly 57%. However, the total return jumps up to 7.6% when we include all distributions that were paid out to shareholders during the same time frame. This is possible because ULTY offers an annual distribution rate of approximately 80%. ULTY offers a weekly distribution and payouts change every single week. So by the time you read this, the annual distribution rate may be a bit different based on the most recent payout.

While this massive distribution rate offers some creative utility around a fund like ULTY, there are clear risks and tradeoffs that investors must consider. For instance, ULTY clearly has a difficult time participating in market growth and the NAV has continued to decay over time. This doesn't inherently mean that you can't make money with ULTY, but it requires precise timing on entry and the most ideal market conditions for this to be an effective investment. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's underlying strategy and holdings that it utilizes to generate such a large amount of income.

Fund Strategy

ULTY has an inception dating back to February 2024 and has a gross expense ratio of 1.24%. ULTY's holdings and option strategy has shifted quite a bit over the last year. When I initially covered ULTY, the fund relied heavily on a few select names. The fund's prospectus states that it utilizes a synthetic option strategy to generate income from its holdings.

To invest synthetically, the Fund will use options contracts on Underlying Securities (considered indirect or synthetic long holdings of the Underlying Securities) to gain exposure to the share price performance of the Underlying Securities. The allocation between direct and indirect (synthetic) long holdings varies based on strategic decisions and market conditions as assessed by the Adviser.

Unlike some other YieldMax funds, ULTY doesn't deploy a purely synthetic option strategy because it actually holds shares in some of the underlying equities that it writes options against. For instance, we can see that Palantir (PLTR) is the largest position at 6.38% of assets. This is followed by other high volatility names like ProShares UltraPro QQQ (TQQQ), Strategy Inc (MSTR), Tesla (TSLA), and Carvana (CVNA) just to name a few. Therefore, I consider ULTY to utilize a bit of a hybrid synthetic approach by replicating a covered call approach through the mixed use of long calls and short puts on the underlying holdings.

It makes sense that ULTY would choose holdings that demonstrate a higher level of volatility. The underlying strategy relies upon higher levels of implied volatility so that the option premiums collected can support the distributions. The higher the premiums collected, the higher the distributions that can be offered to investors. However, this strategy also presents a greater amount of risk of continued drawdowns. Even though ULTY holds the underlying equities, we can see how the price has continued to deteriorate over time.

Over the last year, ULTY's price continues to deteriorate even though the underlying holdings have experienced massive price growth. For instance, PLTR has appreciated by more than 430%. Therefore, investors should have no expectations for ULTY to experience any sort of positive price growth over the long term.

Performance & Risk

The underlying option strategies will always cap the upside growth that can be experienced. This is because the price upside is capped at whatever the selected strike price is. If ULTY holds an asset priced at $100 per share, it may select a strike price of $105 per share. If the underlying asset appreciates up to $120 per share, ULTY's option will be executed at the strike price, which means that all the upside above the strike price is sacrificed in exchange for the premium collected. Even though the upside appreciation is capped, the downside risk is not.

I am pretty bullish on the outlook of the technology sector over the next decade due to the projected growth of AI. It's estimated that the size of the AI market can expand at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. Even though ULTY has exposure to many companies that are involved with different AI technologies, such as NVIDIA (NVDA), ULTY is sure to underperform from a total return perspective because of the capped nature of its option strategy.

Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that ULTY's price has declined by more than 67.7%. When including all distributions, the total return is only about 1.5%. The price decline can be accelerated during periods where ULTY didn't actually generate a sufficient amount of earnings from its option strategy. So how does an investor actually make money with ULTY?

Well, with a distribution so large, ULTY must not be viewed through a traditional lens. I manually added the weekly payouts so far for 2025, which amounts to $2.726 per share. If we include the most recently declared weekly distribution of $0.0945 per share, which isn't listed below, this brings the total up to $2.8205 per share.

  1. There are 52 weeks in a year.
  2. $2.8205 per share represents 16 payments YTD.
  3. ~36 distributions remaining.
  4. $2.8205 per share / ~$6.24 per share (the current price of ULTY) = 45.2% yield paid out so far.

I've talked about this concept of 'house money' in the past, but this is where the cross-point where an investor has received their initial investment back in the form of distributions. With 45.2% paid out so far, an investor can technically continue to hold a position until they've received back over 100% of their investment and this significantly reduces the risk going forward. Of course, this assumes that the price and distribution amounts of ULTY remain stable for the remainder of the year.

There's certainly a risk that ULTY's price continues to decline into a dangerous territory. We've only seen one instance where a YieldMax fund's price deteriorated to the level where a reverse split was needed. This was experienced with YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY) back in February 2024. While this doesn't exactly mean that a reverse split will be the end of ULTY, it will push back the timeline needed to achieve that golden house money status.

So, can you earn a substantial income here with ULTY? You can, but it may not be the best fund for investors that cannot stomach a continued price drop. If you are interested in weekly distributions, I found that YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (YMAX) was a better fit for me. YMAX has a higher total return while also seeing less of a price decline since its inception. YMAX also offers a competitive distribution rate.

Distribution Utility

If you are interested in this weekly paying fund offered by YieldMax, I would suggest that you take a few minutes to go subscribe to their distribution emails. You can do this by filling out their contact form, and you'll need to specifically request to be added to their distribution emails. This will help you stay up to date on all announced distributions, get the latest estimated annual yield, and an estimated breakdown of how much ROC (return of capital) is used to fund the payouts.

As you can see below, the latest distribution is $0.0945 per share and is estimated to be 100% funded by return of capital. Since we are talking about very large annual yields, I think it's better to receive the distributions in return of capital, in my opinion. This is because return of capital distributions aren't technically classified as income and therefore aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis, which allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

So you can technically hold ULTY until your cost basis reaches $0 per share, at which point all distributions received would be classified as long term capital gains. We could argue that funds like ULTY can be tax efficient within a regular taxable brokerage account because of its use of return of capital. The massive distributions received from ULTY have a variety of different use cases. If you are retired, the large distributions from ULTY can support different lifestyle expenses.

If you don't have a need to spend the income, you may consider reinvesting the distributions. I, personally, implement a dividend wheel strategy with my YieldMax funds, by reinvesting the distributions back into some more traditional growth or value-oriented positions that I have, including things like Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM), ASML Holding (ASML), and Target (TGT). This strategy can help you compound shares at a much quicker rate than usual, and the process becomes a lot more effective once you've achieved house money status.

Takeaway

In conclusion, ULTY offers a large distribution rate with weekly payouts for the investors that can handle a larger risk profile. ULTY has suffered from continuous price declines since its inception due to the nature of its synthetic option strategy. The options cap the upside potential, but still exposes ULTY to the full downside risk. Despite having exposure to a ton of different technology holdings, investors shouldn't have any expectation for ULTY to provide capital appreciation. The fund can be efficiently utilized from an income perspective if an investor achieves 'house money' status, by receiving back their initial investment in the form of distributions. At this point, the forward risk is significantly reduced, and you can continue to collect tax efficient income that you can use to pay bills or reinvest back into your portfolio. I maintain my hold rating simply because YMAX is my preferred choice for a weekly paying ETF from YieldMax.

data.title: Diamond Hill Small-Mid Cap Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Markets were mixed in Q1 as the new presidential administration made repeated waves with an avalanche of activity, which left markets uncertain of the likely path forward. For the quarter, US stocks fell -4.7% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index), notching markets’ first quarterly decline since Q3 2023. Mid-cap stocks held up best, falling a more modest -3.4%, while large caps were down -4.5%. Smaller stocks were hit harder, falling -9.5% — a relatively unsurprising development given smaller companies’ shares are generally more volatile as macroeconomic uncertainty increases. From a style perspective, value fared better up and down the cap spectrum, with large-cap value managing a positive gain of +2.1%. Mid-cap value stocks fell -2.1%, compared to -7.1% for their growth counterparts, while small-cap value stocks declined -7.7% versus -11.1% for small growth (all returns as measured by their respective Russell indices). The shift from growth to value was also relatively unsurprising given the flight to safety prompted by tremendous uncertainty around trade and other fiscal policy shifts in the US.

From a sector perspective, likewise, more defensive sectors in the Russell 2500 Index led in the quarter, while cyclical stocks fell out of favor. Utilities stocks were the index’s sole positive sector, up +8.4%. Conversely, technology stocks were hit hardest, falling -16.6%. Consumer discretionary stocks also fell sharply, declining -11.8%, and industrials stocks were down -10.2%.

What began in Q4 — market leadership transitions in anticipation of potential winners and losers from a major shift in executive-level policy direction — vigorously continued in Q1. To some extent, economic data took a backseat in the quarter as investors seemed to focus narrowly on the likeliest implications of major trade policy shifts announced during the quarter by President Trump. Shortly after the quarter concluded, Trump announced a raft of tariffs to go into effect immediately on what he dubbed “Liberation Day.” As has been the case since Trump began increasing tariffs, countries are responding in various ways, with some reaching out to the US to negotiate better rates and others — notably, China — choosing to retaliate with their own high tariff rates. Many companies have preemptively announced significant investments in their US operations — ostensibly moves aimed at avoiding tariffs

themselves. Ultimately, whether the “Liberation Day” moniker goes down in history or infamy remains to be seen. What is clear is markets are very nervous about the president’s apparent determination to wholesale (and possibly singlehandedly) rework the global trade landscape, even with countries that have historically been significant trade allies, like Taiwan. We’ll have more thoughts on potential direction from here later in this communication.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve held rates in March, citing the likelihood tariffs increase inflationary pressures, while cutting its growth expectations for US GDP in 2025. And the US dollar — which one would generally expect to be bolstered relative to other global currencies as tariffs go up (higher prices for US goods increase demand for US dollars with which to pay for them) — counterintuitively fell as tariff language toughened, perhaps signaling investors’ growing concerns tariffs will contribute to a stagflationary US economic environment with high inflation and low or sluggish growth.

Globally, geopolitical uncertainty remains high, though there have been some signs of modest progress toward ending the war between Russia and Ukraine. Following a dramatic fall-out between President Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the US and Ukraine seemed to slowly make their way back toward a minerals and mining deal which would meaningfully increase the US’s economic involvement in Ukraine and potentially pave the way for a broader peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. However, to date, Russian President Vladimir Putin has stymied the US’s attempts to broker a ceasefire between the two countries. War in the Middle East continues, too, as ceasefires between Israel and Hamas and Israel and Lebanon ended and violence across the region re- escalated. The US, too, struck Houthi rebels in Yemen toward the end of the quarter in an attempt to quell attacks on Red Sea shipping lanes.

While volatility is unsettling, it also introduces interesting investing opportunities for those who have been diligent in their research all along. We have no unique insight into how deep this correction will become, whether it will enter bear market territory or how long it will last. However, we know it has helped reset valuations and offered some compelling opportunities to purchase high-quality names at more attractive prices than we’ve seen in some time. Once the trade-related dust settles, time will tell which sectors and individual companies are winners and losers. In the meantime, we will continue deploying our time-tested approach, which is well-built for uncertain market environments like we’re currently seeing — and which we wouldn’t be surprised to see persist for the foreseeable future.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio, though down on an absolute basis, outperformed the Russell 2500 Index in Q1. Relative strength was concentrated among our technology and industrials holdings, both of which held up better than benchmark peers. Our below-benchmark exposure to technology was also a relative tailwind, as were our financials holdings.

Conversely, our materials and energy holdings posed relative headwinds during the quarter, as did our below- benchmark exposure to the utilities sector, which performed well amid investors’ flight to safety.

On an individual holdings basis, top contributors to return in Q1 included Verisign (VRSN) and Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII).

VeriSign, which provides internet infrastructure and domain name registry services, successfully renewed its .com agreement with the US Department of Commerce in late 2024. It also demonstrated stability in both its .com and .net domain bases. We believe the company is favorably positioned with an attractive business model and management team, which has effectively opportunistically allocated capital.

Shares of US Department of Defense and Navy supplier Huntington Ingalls rose in Q1 following President Trump’s announced formation of a new office of shipbuilding. The president further made comments suggesting the government is working to help Huntington Ingalls address recent supply chain and labor issues, which we anticipate will be resolved over time. We maintain our conviction in Huntington Ingalls’ positioning relative to the Department of Defense’s need to prepare for a potential conflict in the Pacific — which would in turn ensure strong demand for the next 5 to 10 years. Further, we believe resolving the aforementioned supply chain and labor issues will allow Huntington Ingalls to improve its margins — a likelihood which we believe is not yet reflected in the share price.

Other top Q1 contributors included Mr. Cooper Group (COOP), WNS Holdings (WNS) and Coterra Energy (CTRA). Mortgage servicing company Mr. Cooper Group executed well during a particularly challenging mortgage environment as interest rates have remained high relative to historical levels. Importantly, Mr. Cooper’s business model is specifically designed to help protect it from just such periods: As rates go up, the value of its mortgage-servicing rights business goes up, giving it the ability to continue generating healthy cash flows even as the mortgage industry overall may slow. Business process management company WNS indicated it expects to return to steady, predictable growth following several quarters of uncharacteristically volatile fundamentals, giving a boost to shares in the quarter. Shares of oil and gas exploration and production company Coterra rose as investors’ optimism about natural gas prices, which rose in the quarter, improved.

Among our bottom individual Q1 contributors to return were Regal Rexnord (RRX) and Live Oak Bancshares (LOB). Power transmission components manufacturer Regal Rexnord faced pressure amid growing tariff-related concerns.

Generally, its organic growth has held up better than peers’, and though its leverage is somewhat elevated, merger synergies and debt paydown rates are beating expectations — factors which should position it well relative to competitors. We believe the recent weakness has been an overreaction and maintain our conviction in our thesis.

Shares of regional bank Live Oak Bancshares were pressured as it faced credit issues during the quarter as well as auditor questions regarding its review process. However, we believe the company has carved out an attractive lending niche to small businesses and could position itself well to become a go-to lender for small businesses in the US.

Other bottom Q1 contributors included WESCO (WCC), Teleflex (TFX) and Ciena (CIEN). Shares of industrial distributor WESCO were pressured amid the company’s cyclical exposure and leveraged balance sheet. However, despite recent mixed fundamentals, we believe the company can still leverage its significant scale advantage to take share and improve margins over time. Further, we expect it to benefit from several secular tailwinds, which should contribute to strong free cash-flow generation, allowing it to deleverage over time.

Teleflex, which designs, develops, manufactures and supplies single-use medical devices for common critical care and surgical diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, declined following its announcement that it would spin off some of its underperforming businesses. Shares of networking systems company Ciena were pressured amid concerns about the durability and timing of cloud customers’ orders.

Portfolio Activity

As volatility picked up sharply in the quarter, we were active in the portfolio — and we anticipate that as volatility continues into Q2, we will likewise attempt to capitalize on compelling opportunities to reposition the portfolio for the period ahead. We initiated three new positions in Q1: Illumina (ILMN), Builders FirstSource (BLDR) and TransUnion (TRU).

Illumina provides sequencing- and array-based solutions for genetic and genomic analysis. It has roughly 80% share in its markets, strong fundamentals and a healthy balance sheet. Looking forward, we believe optionality from new tests other companies run using Illumina products, particularly for cancer screening and detection, should create a long-term growth tailwind. Shares have been pressured recently amid weaker end-market demand, combined with concerns about China, new competition from competitor Roche and the potential for National Institute of Health funding cuts — giving us an opportunity to initiate an investment in a quality business with good long-term growth prospects at the forefront of genetic sequencing.

Builders FirstSource is the US’s leading lumber and building materials distributor, selling lumber and prefabricated components used in home framing, as well as other products and services. Given the large single-family home deficit, we anticipate demand for construction will increase

— and Builders FirstSource is well-positioned, particularly given its innovative digital solutions. We further anticipate the possibility of share buybacks and accretive acquisitions, both of which should enhance shareholder value in the long run.

TransUnion is a global consumer credit reporting agency providing risk and information solutions. Recent concerns about higher interest rates and the risk of recession have weighed on shares, giving us an opportunity to start a position. We believe the market is overly focused on the near term and believe the long-term outlook for this company is solid.

We funded these purchases in part with the proceeds from our sales of regional airlines Allegiant Travel (ALGT) and Alaska Air Group (ALK), timberland real estate investment trust Rayonier (RYN) and electronics component manufacturer Sensata Technologies (ST), which we exited in favor of more compelling investment opportunities. We also exited our position in oil and gas exploration and production company Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY) in favor of better-positioned oil companies.

Finally, we exited our position in Informatica, a comprehensive data management software company providing an array of solutions that facilitate monitoring, manipulating and moving data assets at large enterprises. Following Q4, we had serious questions about management’s ability to execute Informatica’s cloud model transition, as well as about the underlying strength of the business’s installed base. We consequently sold our position to reallocate our capital to names in which we have greater fundamental conviction.

Market Outlook

As we start Q2, uncertainty is as high as it has been, arguably since the pandemic’s earliest days. While markets struggle to digest the likeliest ramifications of the US’s newly announced tariffs — whether they are largely a negotiation tactic whose economic impact will moderate over time as countries and companies reach better terms with the US, or whether they are here to stay for the long term and we will indeed see a significant reshaping of the world trade order — we are watching several factors particularly closely. Foremost, we are monitoring the relationship between the US and China, which is front and center in this trade showdown. As we write, President Trump has threatened a 104% tariff on China in response to its retaliatory increase to 84%. Given the magnitude of the US and Chinese economies, seeing the two countries enter a full-blown trade war would have significant, widespread implications for many US companies, up and down the capitalization spectrum.

Further, the increased uncertainty across a range of macroeconomic policies — trade as well as domestic spending and the administration’s ongoing efforts to meaningfully shrink the size of the federal government — is prompting concerns about the consumer. Should tariffs prompt a significant spike in inflation and/or fiscal spending cuts go too far, consumers could rein in their spending.

Indeed, companies across various sectors have noted a weakening consumer, suggesting the economy is slowing. With the consumer powering a significant majority of the economy, further weakening may present a risk to the lofty double-digit corporate earnings growth the market had been expecting for 2025.

However, regardless of how uncomfortable such volatility can be, we are simultaneously encouraged by its impact on the valuations of some companies that we believe are particularly strong and well-positioned to weather the environment ahead — even if it requires further suffering before economic and market growth resume. Accordingly, we have sought to be active in this period, identifying high- quality companies with particularly solid balance sheets whose outlook over the next three to five years is positive in our view. As downward volatility has continued, we have further acted on our research efforts to introduce these names to the portfolio, with the aim of positioning the portfolio well for whatever the environment gives us in the period ahead — whether there is further downside to endure, or whether markets turn around precipitously. We believe, with hindsight, that this period may prove to be one that separates those investors with a rigorous, bottom-up process from those unwilling or unable to see through the near-term volatility to the possibilities beyond.

Our primary focus is always on achieving value-added results for our existing clients, and we believe we can achieve better-than-market returns over the next five years through active portfolio management.
