data.title: Ternium: A Value Opportunity In The Regional Steel Industry
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Ternium (NYSE:TX) is not just a steel producer: it is a key player in the Latin American industrial sector. With almost twenty years of history, it has achieved something few companies in the sector can do: integrate the entire production chain, from the mine to the final product. This integration not only gives TX greater efficiency and complete control, but also allows it to innovate sustainably, without sacrificing quality.

Q1 2025 was not easy at the sector level, and TX was no exception. Its sales fell 18% YoY, impacted by lower prices and trade uncertainty, particularly in Mexico. Adjusted EBITDA cut down 62% YoY, and margins also contracted. But even in this context, TX showed its ability to adapt: it grew in Brazil and Argentina, and closed Q1 2025 with a solid financial position, with $1.3B in net cash. This speaks not only of resilience, but of a strategy that remains firm, even in difficult times.

If we look at its valuation, the market seems to be missing its full potential. TX is trading with a P/E FWD of just 4.56x and an EV/EBITDA of 4.12x when industry averages are double those values. Its P/B is barely 0.43x, something that occurs in companies in crisis, although TX is not. Quite the opposite: it generates cash, is profitable, and distributes a dividend close to 10%, more than four times the industry average. Furthermore, profits are estimated to recover starting this year, which suggests to me that the market is overcorrecting.

For all this, I consider that TX represents a great opportunity for investors with medium and long-term vision. It is a well-managed, financially sound company with a clear strategy to continue growing in a world that demands more steel but also more sustainability. In short, investing in TX is investing in real industry, in energy transformation, and in a company that not only produces steel, but also the future.

Overview

Ternium is not just a steel company. It is a company deeply rooted in the region, with almost two decades of history building industry, value and community in Latin America. Since its beginning, it has been growing with a clear vision: to integrate the entire steel chain, from ore to final product, with efficiency, innovation and commitment. Today, that vision is materialized in operations spanning Mexico, Brazil, Argentina, Colombia and the US, with plants, service centers, own mines and thousands of people who every day shape that steel that supports infrastructure, industry and the daily lives of millions.

One of the keys to the TX model is its vertical integration. The company not only produces steel: it also extracts the iron ore from which it is manufactured, allowing it to have full control over quality, time, costs and traceability. It is a way of working that prioritizes added value and operational excellence. And that approach takes on a new dimension in places like Pesquería, in northern Mexico, where TX is developing one of its most ambitious projects: a state-of-the-art steelworks with an electric arc furnace (EAF) and a direct reduction iron (DRI) equipped to capture CO2. This new facility, which will be operational by the end of 2026, will not only supply the demanding automotive market with high-end steel, but also meet the origin standards established by the USMCA agreement.

Mexico is the heart of TX's business. It represents almost 50% of its offices and has become a strategic platform to accompany the nearshoring phenomenon. As manufacturing moves from Asia to North America, TX is there, ready to be a key partner in that industrial transformation.

In Brazil, its presence is given through Usiminas (OTCPK:USNZY), one of the great names of steel in that country. With a strong technical orientation, own mineral reserves and a consolidated position in the automotive sector, Usiminas expands the scale and sophistication of the group. In Argentina, the story is different but no less powerful: there, TX integrates into a highly developed value chain, collaborating closely with manufacturers of machinery, household appliances, components for renewable energies and more.

Today, to speak of steel is also to speak of transformation. The global context is changing rapidly: trade tensions, such as the recent rise in US tariffs up to 50% for some imports, reconfigure value chains, demand more flexibility and put pressure on regional producers.

But at the same time, there is a silent revolution going on: the transition to a cleaner industry. TX is not left behind. A concrete target has been set: to reduce its CO2 emissions by 15% per tonne produced by 2030. And this is not just an announcement. It is investing in carbon capture, biomass, hydrogen-based technologies, and renewable energy. In fact, in 2024 it already started operating its first wind farm in Argentina, which today supplies 90% of the electricity from its plants in the country.

In a world that is redefining itself, TX combines the best of two worlds: the industrial strength of a great company and the flexibility of an organization committed to its environment. It's an industry, but it's also a community. It's steel, but it's also the future.

Q1 2025

According to TX data, in Q1 2025 net sales were $3.9B (-18%YoY), mainly explained by lower steel selling prices in all markets, particularly in Mexico, where tariff uncertainty with the US continued to affect purchasing decisions in the commercial segment.

Adjusted EBITDA for the period was $322 MM (-62%YoY), while the adjusted EBITDA margin was 8%, well below the 18% of Q1 2024. Net income was $142 MM vs $491 MM in Q1 2025 and adjusted net profit amounted to $188 MM (-61.7% YoY).

From an operational perspective, steel product shipments totaled 3.86 B tons (-1% YoY), while sales amounted to $3.8B, 19% less than in Q1 2024. Revenue per ton fell by 18%YoY, and operating income reached $276 MM vs $798 MM in Q1 2024, showing a 65% drop.

The results of the steel segment showed a heterogeneous dynamic by region. In Mexico, shipments contracted 8% YoY and the demand remained stable in the industrial segment, while in Brazil, shipments increased 9% YoY, explained by higher local activity and the reactivation of the main Usiminas blast furnace. In the southern region, with a strong presence in Argentina, they increased 32% YoY as a result of the beginning of recovery of industrial activity. Finally, in other markets, sales fell by 14% due to lower US demand.

The mining segment had a positive performance in terms of volume, with 3.06B/ton (+14% YoY) and net sales were $280 MM (+2% YoY). However, the revenue per ton decreased 10% compared to last year due to lower iron ore prices, and the segment's operating result was $57 MM, affected by higher cost per ton which reduced margins.

During the quarter, TX invested $518 MM in capital investments, mainly focused on expanding its industrial center in Pesquería, Mexico. In addition, it ended the quarter with a net cash result of $1.3B, as a result of the strong investment pace.

The operating flow of Q1 2025 was $207 MM, affected by an increase in working capital, mainly due to larger receivables associated with sales growth.

For Q2 2025, TX expects an improvement in its adjusted EBITDA, driven by higher selling prices and a reduction in cost per ton, stable volumes in Brazil and recovery in Argentina due to macroeconomic improvement. In Mexico, activity would continue to be limited by the uncertainty associated with the tariff scheme with the US.

Valuation

TX is undervalued relative to both the materials sector average and its own historical multiples. This is evident in both the P/E FWD of 4.56x and EV/EBITDA of 4.12x, compared to the sector averages of 14.53x and 8.08x, respectively.

In terms of P/E, TX trades at less than a third of its peers' average, while its EV/EBITDA implies that investors are paying very little for future operating cash flow, which, I believe, is more linked to cyclical weakness and fears surrounding emerging markets (such as Mexico and Argentina) than to structural deterioration of the business.

Meanwhile, the P/B of 0.43x suggests that the market is assigning less than half of the book value, which is generally typical of companies in crisis or unprofitable, something that does not apply to TX. The dividend yield of 9.86% reinforces the perception of undervaluation and efficiency in cash generation, with a return well above the sector average (2.16%).

In terms of growth, recent quarters have been challenging. EBITDA fell 71% YoY and EBIT fell 84% YoY, both impacted by lower benchmark steel prices, lower margins, and cost pressures. However, going forward, consensus anticipates a partial normalization: projected EPS for 2025 is more than three times that of 2024, with a slight negative adjustment in 2026 and a further rebound in 2027. In other words, the market expects the bottom line to be behind, validating a positioning strategy at depressed levels.

On the profitability side, TX shows signs of resilience despite the environment. The EBITDA margin is still at 8%, and while this represents a drop from its 5-year average of 22%, it remains positive in a context of global slowdown.

Furthermore, it maintains a projected ROIC of around 14% and a dividend yield close to 10%, well above the industry average. Net cash of $1.3B gives it the financial flexibility to continue investing without compromising its solidity, even with demanding CapEx such as the Pesquería expansion.

The market appears to be penalizing TX for its regional exposure and the cyclical nature of its business, ignoring its low debt level, operational capacity, capital discipline, and strong cash generation. If multiples were to partially converge toward their historical averages, the implied valuation would be much higher than the current market value.

Risks

One of the main factors facing TX is the global economic environment, which continues to show signs of instability. The steel industry is, by nature, cyclical, so that both finished product and raw material prices can vary greatly in short periods, creating a need to maintain very precise cost management, inventory and production efficiency.

On the other hand, the current geopolitical context is changing many of the rules of international trade. Measures such as the increase in tariffs on steel imports by the US have direct impacts on export and import flows, especially in regions where TX has a significant presence. This could affect from competitiveness to medium-term investment planning.

Moreover, TX is exposed to risks associated with emerging economies, as it pertains to countries with complex macroeconomic environments, such as Argentina, Brazil or Mexico, where factors such as inflation, FX variability, trade restrictions or changing regulatory frameworks may influence operational and financial performance.

The ongoing environmental transformation must also be considered. Global pressure to reduce carbon emissions and move towards a cleaner industry is an inevitable path, requiring sustained investment, strategic alliances and market conditions that are still evolving. There is uncertainty about transition costs, the availability of key inputs such as green hydrogen, or the stability of energy and environmental regulatory frameworks.

In short, the risks exist and they are not minor. But the important thing is not to avoid them because you can't, but how they are managed. And in that sense, TX has the experience, scale and equipment necessary to face them with responsibility and long-term vision.

Conclusion

There are companies that survive cycles. And there are others, like TX, that use them to strengthen themselves. While the market scrutinizes short-term noise, TX continues doing what it does best: operating efficiently, investing wisely, and building long-term value.

Its integrated model, from the mine to the final product, allows it to have complete control over costs, quality, and timing. But beyond that, I believe what stands out is its ability to think big: it is investing in clean technology, state-of-the-art plants, and a value chain aligned with the new industry.

It currently trades at rock-bottom multiples: a P/E FWD of 4.5x, EV/EBITDA of 4.1x, and a P/B of 0.43x. All this while distributing a dividend close to 10%. In other words, it generates cash, has room to grow, and is being undervalued by temporary factors rather than structural problems.

Therefore, I believe that for investors with a patient and strategic outlook, TX represents an opportunity to buy a solid, profitable, and well-managed company that the market has not yet fully recognized.

data.title: LGLV: A Defensive Alternative Amid Increased Market Volatility
data.description:
The SPDR SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility Index ETF (LGLV), launched on 02/20/2013 by State Street Global Advisors and managed by SSGA Funds Management, aims to replicate the SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility Index that consists of large-cap stocks issued in the U.S. markets that have exhibited low volatility in the past. It charges an expense ratio of 0.12% and manages about $1 billion.

With trade policy uncertainty and a Fed appearing stuck in inaction as a result, the currently increased levels of volatility are likely to remain so until it's clearer that we're moving towards a resolution. Being defensive is reasonable and to that end, LGLV could serve well investors who still want to participate in the market.

Portfolio

The number of holdings is currently 168 and idiosyncratic risk is very low as the portfolio is weighted based on volatility (lower = greater weight), instead of market capitalization.

Consistent with the low volatility bias, financial institutions have the greatest weight in the fund; their shares often exhibit consistent earnings and low beta. The Industrials sector also has a meaningful weight. Though tech stocks also have a substantial weight, the screen most likely emphasizes firms with strong balance sheets and less cyclicality for them to be considered "low-volatility".

Performance

The price investors pay beyond the fees is what some may consider a "meaningful" return differential when you compare the LGLV's performance against the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

But just to be clear, in the last decade, LGLV hasn't exactly proved to be a good alternative to a vanilla large-cap ETF like SPY on any basis. Its annualized return has been about one percentage point lower, while its standard deviation and maximum drawdown haven't been meaningfully lower. Its Sharpe has been the same as SPY's.

However, one could argue it can be useful during periods of extreme market conditions. Its worst year, which was in 2022, involved an 8.17% decrease. SPY suffered an 18.17% fall in the same year.

It comes down to whether investors are willing to underperform in the long run for those less scary paper losses in adverse conditions. If not, rejecting the ETF as a buy/hold vehicle and using it tactically makes more sense.

Which brings me to the outlook. Let's now see how current conditions shape the value proposition of this fund.

Outlook

Monetary Policy

In March, we got the update to the Summary of Economic Projections, which has the median Fed policymaker forecasting two rate cuts this year, down from three about five months ago. Since then, inflation has been decelerating and real GDP growth has remained solid, with a labor market in an overall decent condition.

So, why the forecast deceleration in rate cuts? Basically, the central bank is reluctant to return to easing as tariffs threaten to reignite upward pressure on the pace of price growth. And tariffs have been imposed too recently for the hard data to reflect their impact.

Soft data, on the other hand, indicate the potential issues that central bankers are wary of. Some of them have commented on how business contacts are ready to hike prices because of tariffs. Yes, growth is at risk too if demand growth decelerates; the consumer-sentiment index from the University of Michigan is at one of the lowest levels recorded going back to 1952.

However, the Fed cannot risk acting preemptively right now and risk undoing the progress on inflation. It did that last year and had to pause the rate cuts as inflation went up again; monetary policy stance volatility is unwelcome by the markets and can cause serious issues when it comes to general economic and market confidence.

So, for now, it's a wait-and-see phase for the Fed. Despite that, I now believe that the two rate cuts later this year can happen. This is not optimistic, but realistic. Tariffs are most likely going to impact inflation in the short term, but it is now at a low enough level for the increase to result in simply less modest inflation levels in the near term, not dangerously high ones. Remember, tariffs won't be accompanied by stimulus (the Fed keeps reducing its Treasury holdings) and the same labor market conditions observed back in 2020-2021.

In any case, while the Fed may start cutting after summer, it is unlikely to do so later this month. This might not be well received by the markets, although they mostly expect it. Having low-volatility holdings in one's portfolio seems reasonable for now.

Trade Policy

The most serious issue right now is what the final trade agreement between the U.S. and China will look like. It's highly unlikely that tariffs will return to triple-digit rates; that was just political posturing from both sides.

However, the current precarious state of the agreement, as both parties have continued to engage in a trade war even without resorting to tariffs, implies market volatility ahead. That's where ETFs such as LGLV can be useful.

Moreover, the trade friction between the European Union and the United States can add to the unpredictability relating to growth and inflation, as it is yet unclear where this is going exactly. President Trump's negotiating power has been weakened by a recent trade court ruling deeming most of his tariffs illegal. But he was still able to and did raise the tariffs on steel and aluminum to 50% recently, which has already made the E.U. state it is ready to react with countermeasures.

Sentiment & Positioning

The NAAIM exposure index, which shows how many active portfolio managers are bullish on U.S. equity stocks based on current allocations, quickly went up in the last month. However, the AAII allocation survey still shows that retail investors are relatively modestly positioned. This positioning divergence is reflective of a cautious approach, but is a bullish signal since professionals are positioned for upside.

However, there is a problem. Around the time managers started reallocating to equities, this is when it seems it was a good time to buy in retrospect, as the market climbed above its 50DMA a couple of weeks later. The current momentum appears to be high enough for professionals to maintain their exposure. But retail investors are skeptical and this is justified given current levels amid high economic uncertainty.

So, it's not surprising that in the past 6 months net fund flows for LGLV have been positive at $236.18 million and negative for SPY at $-14.57 billion. Investors have been and seem to remain defensive.

All in all, current conditions and sentiment indicate that a defensive position is justified. For this reason, LGLV is now likely to do better than the market as long as the uncertainty persists.

Your Turn

What do you think? Do you agree with this thesis or not? Let me know below.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Avista: Maybe Boring Utility Returns, But Not With This Strategy
data.description:
Article Thesis

I initially covered Avista Corporation (NYSE:AVA) in October last year, when I assigned it a “Buy” rating, with the stock at ~$38. My Fair Value estimate was $43.07, based on a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) and backed by comparisons with peers and with Avista’s five-year averages.

Avista is mostly a regulated utility, and utility stocks are usually boring, with some exceptions like 2024. That’s why I generally initiate a full position in a utility stock if the sum undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth is higher than 20%.

However, by using the power of options, I can have significant leverage. In this case, I sold a Put Spread, then transformed into a single Put. Like mentioned in my second article, this strategy brought about 44% return in about 5 months, compared to a single-digit return for the stock. With the stock around $40 in March and a Fair Value estimate of $42.56, Avista did not satisfy anymore my rule of 20% mentioned above for utilities.

Like mentioned in the second article, I was waiting for the stock to drop under $38 again. As posted through a note on May 14, I re-initiated a position at ~$37.5. This time I sold only a Put, but I do have the intention to transform into a Put Spread again. More about this strategy later.

Last Quarter Highlights

Avista is mostly a regulated utility (90% - 95% of their business) with operations in 4 states:

We can observe that in Washington they have new rates effective January 2025, for a ROE (Return On Equity) of 9.8%. For both Idaho and Oregon, they are expecting new rates to be settled in September this year, for proposed ROE of 10.4% and 9.5%.

The problem is that Avista has a more modest realized ROE of 7.27%, influenced by their unregulated part of the business, which is more exposed to short-term market price gyrations. Long-term, they do expect to see a return on these investments, and compared to 7.1% ROE in March, the trend is encouraging.

Q1’2025 EPS was $0.98, compared to $0.91 one year ago. For FY’25 they are reaffirming EPS guidance for $2.52 - $2.72, while consensus EPS is $2.56, a remarkable 11% YoY growth. However, this growth is partially derived from the new rates and from the losses incurred in 2024 in their unregulated segment.

According to Avista’s expected capital spend for 2025-2029, they continue to expect an annual rate base growth of 5% - 6%, while there are other U.S. regulated utilities expecting 7% - 9%, especially some Southern ones, which are favored by increased demand of electricity brought by the Data Center boom, but not only.

Avista maintains a BBB credit rating, stable outlook, from S&P Global. However, for comparison, stronger U.S. regulated utilities usually have a BBB+ credit rating.

On the other side of the capital structure, they issued $16M new equity in Q1’25 from a total of $80M expected for 2025. This will result in marginal ~0.5% dilution, which I consider normal for a utility.

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, let’s start with a comparison with Avista’s five-year averages:

For each industry, I look at the most relevant multiples, and for regulated utilities I think these are:

  • Price / Book (closer to Price / Rate Base): 1.17, compared to 1.28 5Y, denotes ~10% undervaluation.
  • EV / EBITDA (because it includes both profitability and debt, which are crucial for utilities): 10.06, compared to 11.72 5Y, implies about 16% undervaluation.

If we look at the same two multiples in comparison with the sector:

  • EV / EBITDA gives about the same undervaluation.
  • Price Book: 1.17, compared to 1.85 for the sector, would give us a more significant undervaluation. However, this is not very reliable in this form, it’s important to look at this multiple together with ROE and, like I mentioned before, Avista has a lower ROE than other regulated utilities.

For an absolute valuation, I use a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) in two stages. For the first five years, I think that consensus dividend estimates are a little too optimistic:

That’s because they raised the dividend only by 3.2% in February, due to a high payout ratio (about 75% expected for 2025), and they will keep increasing the dividend at a slower pace than their EPS, trying to bring down their payout ratio to about 70%. That’s why I’ll model only 3.5% growth:

Fair Value

My Fair Value estimate is $42.12, about 10% potential upside from the current price ($38.29), while the relative valuation gives about (or at least) the same numbers: 10%-16% potential upside.

If we add a 5.2% dividend yield and 3.2% dividend growth, we get a total of 20.3%, only marginally above my 20% threshold. That’s why I assign it a “Buy” status under $38.

A Balanced Leveraged Strategy For a Boring Utility

Since I consider it as a “Buy” under $38, as posted in my note from May 14, with the stock at ~$37.5, I sold a 35 Put expiring December ’25, for $218.

However, there are some important differences between a “cash-type” account and a “margin-type” account:

Cash account: I need to set aside $3500 (since I could be assigned). Subtracting the collected premium, we have a cost of $3282, and a maximum ROI of 218 / 3282 = 6.6%, or about 12% annualized. A covered Call (100 shares and a short Call) gives slightly better results in this period, with the Call prices higher than Put prices due to high interest rates.

Margin account: I am required to cover only the margin; however I like to be more prudent, I want to set aside more cash. I usually look at what I think to be the worst-case scenario. I can’t see Avista under $25 (more about risks later) until December. At $25, they would have a 7.8% dividend yield, outstanding for a regulated utility, even if there will be no growth.

So, I set aside $1000 (3500 – 2500). If we subtract the collected premium of $218, we have a cost of $782 and a maximum ROI of ~28%, or about ~52% annualized. This is significantly more than the return in a cash account. Yes, for other stocks we could have even more, but also with a higher risk than for a regulated utility.

Some possible outcomes at expiration:

As usual, I will very likely close the position before expiration, with little extrinsic value left. For example, if the Put will value only $50 in September, it’s not worth it to keep it another three months, to avoid a price reversal.

Full position: However, this Put is not enough for a full position, I will likely complete it with some shares. There are several reasons for that:

  • Avista has very illiquid options; I got a good price on May 14, but I might not be able to get it again, even with the stock under $38, especially with some time passing.
  • As you can see in the table above, in the unlikely scenario with Avista going towards $30 (and especially below $30), the Put loss (in a margin account) is higher than the stock loss.
  • The stock also performs better than the Put at ~$50 or above (even if another unlikely scenario).

So, I’m watching closely these days if I can buy some shares under $38, but still not for a full position.

Another possibility is to combine these new shares with buying a 30 Put, so transforming the short 35 Put into a short 35/30 Put Spread. But again, it depends on the premium that I could get. If I have to pay $100 for the 30 Put, that means a collected premium of 218 – 100 = $118. With a cost of 500 (cash needed to set aside if both Puts will finish In-The-Money) – 118 = $382, maximum ROI would be 118 / 382 = ~30%. So, any price lower than $100 for the 30 Put would be interesting, with better returns from the Put Spread than from the short 35 Put.

And I will initiate a third round (shares or options) only if the stock will go down more, without major fundamental shifts (averaging down).

Risks

There is a bit of uncertainty about their pending rate cases in Idaho and Oregon, let’s suppose they won’t get a favorable rate. Coupled with the more capricious unregulated business segment, let’s say they will need to slow down even more their dividend growth. With 2.5% dividend growth for the next 5 years, Fair Value would be $40.3, still very slightly undervalued, but less than my 20% threshold for a new position. So, I won’t increase my position, and I won’t renew my Put. However, I would keep my existing position (considering I’ll acquire some shares in the meantime), for which I require only 10% combined undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth.

Can I be assigned for the short Put before expiration? Yes, that can happen occasionally, if the position is ITM (In-The-Money), of course, meaning the stock under $35, and I could get 100 shares, which will drag $3500, significantly more than the $1000 that I set aside. But that’s very simple, I will instantly close the 100 shares after the assignment notice and roll over another Put.

Related to the cash needed to set aside for the Put option, I already said that I tried to imagine the worst-case scenario until December, with Avista at $25. Now, can the things be even worse? Even highly unlikely, anything can happen in theory. For example, wildfires are a risk especially with the summer coming, but Avista has wildfire mitigation plans, together with the authorities from Washington or Idaho, so I don’t think that could be such a catastrophic event.

However, let’s suppose that the unimaginable will somehow happen and Avista will go below $25 until December. We need to look at this in the dynamics of a portfolio. I have several short Put positions and a total cash and equivalents position which is significantly greater than the sum of cash set aside for these Put positions. So, if one of these short Put will go below my worst-case scenario, it won’t affect too much my entire portfolio.

Takeaway

Avista is mostly a regulated utility business, with increased rates in 2025 in Washington and pending rate cases in Idaho and Oregon. A 5.1% dividend yield and modest 3% - 3.5% dividend growth support my investment thesis, despite lower ROE versus peers.

Derived from a DDM and backed by comparisons with Avista's 5-year averages and with the sector, my Fair Value estimate is $42.12, offering ~10% potential upside, besides the dividend. With a total undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth = 20.3%, I rate the stock a "Buy" under $38.

For more appealing returns, I sold a $35 Put Dec'25, which I expect to yield a maximum of ~28% ROI, or 52% annualized (in a margin account, considering the worst-possible case with Avista at $25 until December).

I am planning to transform this Put into a Put Spread by buying a $30 Put if I can get a favorable price, and also to add some shares, partly on another pullback under $38, partly only on a bigger drop.
